title: RAG Fundamentals
description: Retrieval pipelines, chunking, reranking, grounding, citations, and RAG evaluation.

questions.title: When would you use RAG instead of fine-tuning?
questions.scenario: A support knowledge team runs RAG assistant for troubleshooting articles, policies, and release notes. A recent review found that the model generates confident answers from weak or stale retrieved context; the baseline fails on 20-35% of reviewed cases, so the team needs a reliable approach for RAG instead of fine-tuning measured by context recall, context precision, faithfulness, and answer acceptance.
questions.expectedAnswer: RAG instead of fine-tuning requires comparing at least two viable choices instead of assuming one technique is universally better. In RAG assistant for troubleshooting articles, policies, and release notes, the practical approach is retrieval grounding with chunking, hybrid search, reranking, citations, and faithfulness checks. The first step is to establish a baseline with representative examples from parsed docs, chunks, embeddings, citations, answer traces, and feedback labels, because without a baseline the team cannot distinguish real improvement from a better-looking demo. Next, implement the smallest production-shaped change using Milvus or pgvector, OpenSearch BM25, a cross-encoder reranker, and Ragas-style evaluation, keeping enough trace data to inspect inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures. The main risk is retrieving plausible but unsupported context; a strong design makes that risk visible through tests, monitoring, and review workflows. Validation should use context recall, context precision, faithfulness, and answer acceptance, segmented by important user or data groups so averages do not hide regressions. The tradeoff is that stronger controls add complexity, latency, and ownership work, but they also reduce silent failure in production. A good interview answer explains the mechanism, the implementation path, the measurable success criteria, and the condition under which a simpler baseline is preferable.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: The scenario points to retrieving plausible but unsupported context. RAG instead of fine-tuning addresses that risk through retrieval grounding with chunking, hybrid search, reranking, citations, and faithfulness checks, so the discussion should start with the observed production failure rather than a textbook definition. The first concrete step is to turn recent failures into a benchmark and compare the baseline against the proposed change using context recall, context precision, faithfulness, and answer acceptance.
answerFramework.howToApply: Start with 50-200 representative examples from parsed docs, chunks, embeddings, citations, answer traces, and feedback labels. Implement the change with Milvus or pgvector, OpenSearch BM25, a cross-encoder reranker, and Ragas-style evaluation, keeping the old path available as a baseline. Log inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures so the team can debug regressions. Roll out behind a feature flag or pilot group, then expand only if quality and operational metrics improve.
answerFramework.example: Example: in RAG assistant for troubleshooting articles, policies, and release notes, the baseline path accepts an input, produces a plausible output, but leaves no reliable evidence that the decision is correct. The improved path adds retrieval grounding with chunking, hybrid search, reranking, citations, and faithfulness checks; it records the evidence, score, trace, or review status that supports the output. The final result is tied to validated signals instead of model confidence or notebook accuracy alone.
answerFramework.tradeoffs: The technique adds engineering overhead, evaluation work, and usually some latency or infrastructure cost. It is worthwhile when the failure affects trust, safety, revenue, or repeated manual effort. It is not worth overengineering for rare, low-risk tasks where a simple baseline already meets the target. Monitor context recall, context precision, faithfulness, and answer acceptance so the team can tell whether the extra complexity pays for itself.

relatedQuestions.title: Walk through a basic RAG pipeline.

commonMistakes: Explaining RAG instead of fine-tuning as a memorized definition without tying it to a production failure mode.
Skipping measurement and relying on anecdotal demo quality instead of context recall, context precision, faithfulness, and answer acceptance.
Ignoring the main risk: retrieving plausible but unsupported context.
Not discussing ownership, rollout, monitoring, and rollback when the approach fails in production.
followUpQuestions: What metric would prove that RAG instead of fine-tuning improved the system rather than just changed it?
What failure mode would you expect after deploying RAG instead of fine-tuning, and how would you detect it?
How would your answer change if latency, cost, or compliance became the primary constraint?
questions.realWorldExample: A support knowledge team applied RAG instead of fine-tuning in RAG assistant for troubleshooting articles, policies, and release notes. The baseline suffered from retrieving plausible but unsupported context, causing roughly 25% of reviewed cases to require manual correction. The team implemented retrieval grounding with chunking, hybrid search, reranking, citations, and faithfulness checks using Milvus or pgvector, OpenSearch BM25, a cross-encoder reranker, and Ragas-style evaluation, then evaluated the change against context recall, context precision, faithfulness, and answer acceptance. After a limited rollout, manual corrections dropped by 12-18 percentage points and p95 latency stayed within the agreed operating budget.
questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate gives a concrete mechanism for RAG instead of fine-tuning, names the data and metrics needed to validate it, and explains the operational risk. A great candidate also discusses rollout, observability, ownership, and when the added complexity is not justified.

questions.title: Walk through a basic RAG pipeline.
questions.scenario: A support knowledge team runs RAG assistant for troubleshooting articles, policies, and release notes. A recent review found that the model generates confident answers from weak or stale retrieved context; the baseline fails on 20-35% of reviewed cases, so the team needs a reliable approach for basic RAG pipeline measured by context recall, context precision, faithfulness, and answer acceptance.
questions.expectedAnswer: basic RAG pipeline is important because it changes how the system is designed, measured, and trusted in production. In RAG assistant for troubleshooting articles, policies, and release notes, the practical approach is document ingestion, parsing, chunking, embedding, hybrid retrieval, reranking, prompt assembly, generation, citation, and feedback capture. The first step is to establish a baseline with representative examples from parsed docs, chunks, embeddings, citations, answer traces, and feedback labels, because without a baseline the team cannot distinguish real improvement from a better-looking demo. Validation should use context recall, context precision, faithfulness, and answer acceptance, segmented by important user or data groups so averages do not hide regressions. The tradeoff is that stronger controls add complexity, latency, and ownership work, but they also reduce silent failure in production. questions.title: How would you explain retrieval recall in a RAG Fundamentals interview?
questions.scenario: A support knowledge team runs RAG assistant for troubleshooting articles, policies, and release notes. A recent review found that the model generates confident answers from weak or stale retrieved context; the baseline fails on 20-35% of reviewed cases, so the team needs a reliable approach for retrieval recall measured by context recall, context precision, faithfulness, and answer acceptance.
questions.expectedAnswer: retrieval recall requires comparing at least two viable choices instead of assuming one technique is universally better. In RAG assistant for troubleshooting articles, policies, and release notes, the practical approach is metric selection based on false-positive and false-negative business cost, threshold tuning, and segmented evaluation. The first step is to establish a baseline with representative examples from parsed docs, chunks, embeddings, citations, answer traces, and feedback labels, because without a baseline the team cannot distinguish real improvement from a better-looking demo. Next, implement the smallest production-shaped change using scikit-learn, confusion matrices, PR curves, ROC curves, and calibration plots, keeping enough trace data to inspect inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures. The main risk is optimizing a metric that does not match the business decision; a strong design makes that risk visible through tests, monitoring, and review workflows. Validation should use context recall, context precision, faithfulness, and answer acceptance, segmented by important user or data groups so averages do not hide regressions. The tradeoff is that stronger controls add complexity, latency, and ownership work, but they also reduce silent failure in production. A good interview answer explains the mechanism, the implementation path, the measurable success criteria, and the condition under which a simpler baseline is preferable.

questions.title: How would you explain RAG observability in a RAG Fundamentals interview?
questions.scenario: A support knowledge team runs RAG assistant for troubleshooting articles, policies, and release notes. A recent review found that the model generates confident answers from weak or stale retrieved context; the baseline fails on 20-35% of reviewed cases, so the team needs a reliable approach for RAG observability measured by context recall, context precision, faithfulness, and answer acceptance.
questions.expectedAnswer: RAG observability belongs in the architecture discussion because it affects ownership boundaries, controls, observability, and rollout. In RAG assistant for troubleshooting articles, policies, and release notes, the practical approach is retrieval grounding with chunking, hybrid search, reranking, citations, and faithfulness checks. The first step is to establish a baseline with representative examples from parsed docs, chunks, embeddings, citations, answer traces, and feedback labels, because without a baseline the team cannot distinguish real improvement from a better-looking demo. Next, implement the smallest production-shaped change using Milvus or pgvector, OpenSearch BM25, a cross-encoder reranker, and Ragas-style evaluation, keeping enough trace data to inspect inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures. The main risk is retrieving plausible but unsupported context; a strong design makes that risk visible through tests, monitoring, and review workflows. Validation should use context recall, context precision, faithfulness, and answer acceptance, segmented by important user or data groups so averages do not hide regressions. The tradeoff is that stronger controls add complexity, latency, and ownership work, but they also reduce silent failure in production. A good interview answer explains the mechanism, the implementation path, the measurable success criteria, and the condition under which a simpler baseline is preferable.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: The scenario points to retrieving plausible but unsupported context. RAG observability addresses that risk through retrieval grounding with chunking, hybrid search, reranking, citations, and faithfulness checks, so the discussion should start with the observed production failure rather than a textbook definition. The first concrete step is to turn recent failures into a benchmark and compare the baseline against the proposed change using context recall, context precision, faithfulness, and answer acceptance.
answerFramework.howToApply: Start with 50-200 representative examples from parsed docs, chunks, embeddings, citations, answer traces, and feedback labels. Implement the change with Milvus or pgvector, OpenSearch BM25, a cross-encoder reranker, and Ragas-style evaluation, keeping the old path available as a baseline. Log inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures so the team can debug regressions. Roll out behind a feature flag or pilot group, then expand only if quality and operational metrics improve.
answerFramework.example: Example: in RAG assistant for troubleshooting articles, policies, and release notes, the baseline path accepts an input, produces a plausible output, but leaves no reliable evidence that the decision is correct. The improved path adds retrieval grounding with chunking, hybrid search, reranking, citations, and faithfulness checks; it records the evidence, score, trace, or review status that supports the output. The final result is tied to validated signals instead of model confidence or notebook accuracy alone.
answerFramework.tradeoffs: The technique adds engineering overhead, evaluation work, and usually some latency or infrastructure cost. It is worthwhile when the failure affects trust, safety, revenue, or repeated manual effort. It is not worth overengineering for rare, low-risk tasks where a simple baseline already meets the target. Monitor context recall, context precision, faithfulness, and answer acceptance so the team can tell whether the extra complexity pays for itself.

commonMistakes: Explaining RAG observability as a memorized definition without tying it to a production failure mode.
Skipping measurement and relying on anecdotal demo quality instead of context recall, context precision, faithfulness, and answer acceptance.
Ignoring the main risk: retrieving plausible but unsupported context.
Not discussing ownership, rollout, monitoring, and rollback when the approach fails in production.
followUpQuestions: What metric would prove that RAG observability improved the system rather than just changed it?
What failure mode would you expect after deploying RAG observability, and how would you detect it?
How would your answer change if latency, cost, or compliance became the primary constraint?
questions.realWorldExample: A support knowledge team applied RAG observability in RAG assistant for troubleshooting articles, policies, and release notes. The baseline suffered from retrieving plausible but unsupported context, causing roughly 25% of reviewed cases to require manual correction. The team implemented retrieval grounding with chunking, hybrid search, reranking, citations, and faithfulness checks using Milvus or pgvector, OpenSearch BM25, a cross-encoder reranker, and Ragas-style evaluation, then evaluated the change against context recall, context precision, faithfulness, and answer acceptance. After a limited rollout, manual corrections dropped by 12-18 percentage points and p95 latency stayed within the agreed operating budget.
questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate gives a concrete mechanism for RAG observability, names the data and metrics needed to validate it, and explains the operational risk. A great candidate also discusses rollout, observability, ownership, and when the added complexity is not justified.
